purpose: Daily study recommendation should be LLM-curated from derived inventory, progress, and user intent, not merely a fixed topic-pool rotation.
selection_principles: Reflect already studied topics from config/study-progress.json and sources/fos-study scan.
If a candidate topic is already covered by an existing fos-study document, classify it as review/update-existing rather than a new study topic.
Use config pools only as candidates, pins, exclusions, and guardrails; do not treat them as the canonical study inventory.
Prefer topics that connect backend experience to near-term interview answers without copying private application material into public study topics.
Avoid treating generic DB tuning as the active weak area unless the specific topic is useful for the current interview arc.
Keep enough variety across backend depth, commerce domain, operations, architecture, AI/backend tooling, and live-coding practice.
Explain why each recommendation is useful now and what was intentionally skipped.
interest_axes: JPA/MyBatis query and legacy coexistence as interview context, not as a generic weakness
Redis/cache consistency and operational failure modes
observability, incident response, and non-technical communication
AI agent workflow and backend productivity
constraints: Do not generate study packs automatically before the user selects a topic.
Keep public fos-study topics company-neutral unless the user explicitly asks for a private application asset.
Do not claim production agent-operation experience beyond evidence in candidate-profile and approved application files.
Treat LLM agent frameworks and evaluation frameworks as gap-closing study topics unless direct evidence is added.
avoid: Repeating the same DB/Spring/cache pool simply because it remains in config.
Presenting old target-specific material as a fresh study topic when the real value is a short refresh or target-company translation.
Over-indexing on old company context unless explicitly comparing.
